narrative: As a white male applicant with a lower-middle family income background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model looked at key aspects of your academic record, test scores, and personal circumstances to forecast your bar exam success. For you specifically, it estimated a 76% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt—a high-risk outlook that led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The decision hinged on a few standout factors from your profile that most strongly shaped this prediction. Most influential was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is in the bottom tier compared to the typical student's midpoint around 5.5; this low performance signaled to the model a concerning trend in your later undergraduate grades, strongly pushing the prediction toward bar exam failure. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 25, well below the average of about 37 seen in the dataset, further reinforcing the model's concern by highlighting a gap in standardized test readiness essential for bar success. On a more positive note, your lower-middle family income actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction slightly toward passing, as the model associates such backgrounds with resilience in some successful cases. Compared to typical students, who often enter with stronger mid-range deciles, higher LSATs around 37, and varied income levels averaging higher, your profile stood out as riskier in these core academic areas. While being a full-time student aligns with the vast majority at 93%, it didn't sway the outcome much here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character, but a data-driven flag on bar passage odds. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and gathering letters highlighting your determination from your family background. Reapplying after addressing these could shift your predicted odds significantly.
